strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

PERSONAL TRAINING CLIENT INTAKE FORM
Please fill in and save this form in Word, Google Docs or ODS.
The more information you provide, the better I will be able to assist you.
You can use either the imperial or metric system to fill in this form. Just
state which unit you used (e.g. cm, kg, lb, inch).

General
● Full legal name: Frank
● Street and house number: ...
● Postal code: ...
● City: Amsterdam
● Country: Netherlands
● Age: 26
● Height: 172 cm
● Weight: 55 kg
● Body fat percentage (plus estimation method): 11.4% (according to
my cousin, who measured me with skin calipers.
● Years of training experience: None

Strength
For the section below, please give your current maximum abilities in
weight times reps, e.g. 250 lb x 6.
● Bench press: Not sure, but I had trouble lifting the bar multiple
times when I worked out with my cousin in the gym.
● Squat (excluding body weight): I just got my squat rack delivered at
home, but haven't had the chance to try it out.
● Chin-up (excluding body weight): If I use my legs a little bit I can
do one pull-up with my palms facing me.
● Overhead press: Not sure.
headings: PERSONAL TRAINING CLIENT INTAKE FORM
General
Strength

pages.text:
BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

What are your fitness goals?
I want to bulk up and gain muscle size. It doesn't really matter if I put on
some fat in the process. Just want to fill out my T-shirts.

How often in a week would you be prepared to train for maximal
results?
I'm very determined to reach this goal, so I would be prepared to train
every single day if necessary.

What is your occupation?
You may be general here, e.g. student/manager/personal trainer/manual
labor/doctor. The aim is to characterize your circadian rhythm, stress and
activity level.
I'm in med school, now starting my internships in multiple specialties.

Please list any pathologies and injuries (disabilities, allergies,
illnesses, syndromes, disorders etc.) you have or had:
I have asthma, but I imagine this won't hold me back in weight training.

Do you insist on any special diet (e.g. ketogenic, vegan)?
I generally find it quite hard to eat enough, so anything that doesn't
involve eating chicken and broccoli 6x a day, which I don't find that
appetizing, is fine.
headings: What are your fitness goals?
How often in a week would you be prepared to train for maximal
results?
What is your occupation?
Please list any pathologies and injuries (disabilities, allergies,
illnesses, syndromes, disorders etc.) you have or had:
Do you insist on any special diet (e.g. ketogenic, vegan)?

pages.text:
BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

Do you plan to use any PEDs while working together? If so, please
list them:
Not sure what a PED is. If you mean supplements, I'm willing to invest in
anything that helps me gain muscle weight.

Lifestyle
Are there any times at which you are unable or unwilling to train?
(This includes when you're at work!) If so, please list them:
I can only workout at night or very early in the morning because of
internship demands. On the weekends I have some more time flexibility.

Activity level
Please mark one of the fields below or delete the rest.
● Sedentary (e.g. office job)

Stress level

How is your sleep rhythm and quality?
I take great care to sleep enough, as I very easily notice it when I don't.

How much caffeine do you consume daily on average or on a
typical work day?
I drink about 3-4 cups of coffee to get through the day.

Equipment
Do you have skinfold calipers and if so, which one(s)?
My cousin has one, and he is willing to regularly measure me.

What are the lightest barbell weight plates you have access to?
I have ordered micro-plates with the squat rack, as my cousin
recommended. These are 500 g each.

Does your available equipment differ from that in most gyms in
any other way (e.g. you train in a Crossfit box or at home)?
I train exclusively at home.

Supplements
Please list all the supplements you are currently taking:
Not any yet.

Genetics
● Wrist circumference (smallest point): 16.5 cm
● Ankle circumference (smallest point): 21 cm
● How much did you weigh before you started lifting and what was
your height at that point? Right now I weigh 55 kg. So that would
be about my pre-lifting weight.
● Were you heavy as a baby? Just asked my dad, and he said I was a
light-weight baby.
● Please attach a picture to this form in which the back of your right
hand is clearly visible. Ideally, place your right hand on a flat
surface with your fingers touching each other and then take a
picture of it.

Current Program
Please describe or attach your current diet plan in detail:

Supplements

Current Program

pages.text:
BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

I currently eat whenever I'm hungry. Most of the time hot-dogs, warm-up
bakeries, and snacks like M&Ms, mini-snickers, pringles. My cousin told
me I should eat a lot of protein for breakfast, so I started eating Quark
instead of milk with my cornflakes (honey pops) in the morning.

Please describe or attach your current training program in detail:
None yet.

Frank is a very light-weight novice. Genetic potential isn't too good,
looking at his wrist (16.5 cm) and ankle (21 cm) circumference. Very
skeptical about the 11.4% body fat percentage, as we have no
information on whether the cousin is competent at skin caliper
measurements.

Because Frank is able to train every day, and because it seems he doesn't
have any substantial knowledge about nutrition, calorie cycling wouldn't
be a wise choice. Hence we want to have him in his anabolic window all of
the time.
Because he wants to train every day, but his anabolic window as an
untrained individual likely lasts about 48-72 h (looking at his low-stress
but high time demand internship, and the fact he puts great value on
sleeping), a push-pull-legs split workout program seems to be the best
choice. This allows him to train almost every day (6 days a week, mental
benefits), while keeping his muscle frequency at a little over 72 h.
Because untrained individuals often experience debilitating soreness that
lasts over 48 h, this would be the best choice. The one rest day per
week would allow him some flexibility for scheduling his training
sessions, and extra time for recuperating from the soreness that is
likely to happen during the first few weeks.

Because he's a complete novice, 9-14 sets per muscle group per week are
headings: sleeping), a push-pull-legs split workout program seems to be the best
lasts over 48 h, this would be the best choice. The one rest day per
week would allow him some flexibility for scheduling his training
sessions, and extra time for recuperating from the soreness that is
likely to happen during the first few weeks.

pages.text:
BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

likely enough. This makes for very short workouts.
However, we want to limit the amount of muscle damage, as we want as
much of the Muscle Protein Synthesis in the Anabolic Windows left for
making the muscles bigger, instead of just repairing them.
To do so, we will start slowly with very light weights, high reps, and at the
lower end of the optimal amount of sets per week.

We will do 1RM estimations via the Epley formula (E1RM = (reps x
weight lifted x 0.033) + weight lifted). This works best by
performing ~3RM for compound exercises and ~5-6RM for
isolation exercises, and plugging those numbers into the formula.
After we've estimated Frank's 1RMs, we take 60% of that as the
working weight for his sets. That's because he is untrained, after
all. And research shows 60%1RM is sufficient for strength and
muscle adaptations at this training status.

Day A (Upper Push) (2x/ week)
4 x 60% (weighted) Push-Up - 1 Chest, 1 Front Delts, ½ Triceps
2 x 60% DB Overhead Triceps Extension - 1 Triceps
4 x 60% Plank - 1 Abs (needs some isometric ab work to learn to control
his core rigidity and lumbopelvic movement)

Day B (Upper Pull) (2x/week)
4 x 60% TRX Face Pull - 1 Rear Delts, 1 Mid/Upper Traps, ½ Lateral
Delts, ½ Lower Traps, ½ Biceps
4 x 60% TRX Close-Grip Row - 1 Lats, ½ Lower Traps, ½ Rear Delts,
½ Biceps
4 x 60% DB Lateral Raise - 1 Lateral Delts

Day C (Lower) (2x/week)
4 x 60% Squat - 1 Quads, 1 Spinal Erectors, 1 Glutes
4 x 60% TRX Leg Curl - 1 Hamstrings, ¼ Glutes
headings: We will do 1RM estimations via the Epley formula (E1RM = (reps x

isolation exercises, and plugging those numbers into the formula.

working weight for his sets. That’s because he is untrained, after
all. And research shows 60%1RM is sufficient for strength and
muscle adaptations at this training status.

4 x 60% (weighted) Push-Up - 1 Chest, 1 Front Delts, ½ Triceps
2 x 60% DB Overhead Triceps Extension - 1 Triceps
4 x 60% Plank - 1 Abs (needs some isometric ab work to learn to control
Day B (Upper Pull) (2x/week)
4 x 60% TRX Face Pull - 1 Rear Delts, 1 Mid/Upper Traps, ½ Lateral
Delts, ½ Lower Traps, ½ Biceps
4 x 60% TRX Close-Grip Row - 1 Lats, ½ Lower Traps, ½ Rear Delts,
½ Biceps
4 x 60% DB Lateral Raise - 1 Lateral Delts
Day C (Lower) (2x/week)
4 x 60% Squat - 1 Quads, 1 Spinal Erectors, 1 Glutes
4 x 60% TRX Leg Curl - 1 Hamstrings, ¼ Glutes

pages.text:
BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

5 x 60% Single-leg Calf Raise - 1 Gastrocs, 1 Soleus

Weekly volumes:
Chest - 8
Front Delts - 8
Triceps - 8
Abs - 8

Lats
Mid/Upper Traps - 8
Lower Traps - 8
Rear Delts - 12
Lateral Delts - 8
Biceps - 8

Quads - 8
Hamstrings - 8
Gastrocs - 10
Glutes - 10
Soleus - 10
Spinal Erectors - 8

This puts most of his muscles at the lower end of the 9-12 sets
recommendation for novices. Gastrocs and soleus a little higher because
they often need a little more volume, while some overemphasis on the
rear delts (as opposed to the front delts, which is often the case) with 12
sets/week is arguably a bad thing. The 10 sets per week for the Glutes are
an inevitable byproduct from Squatting and TRX Leg Curls, but are still
within the recommended range of 9-12 sets per week for untrained/novice
individuals.

Starting at very low starting weights, Frank wants to add 1-2.5 kg to all
headings: 5 x 60% Single-leg Calf Raise - 1 Gastrocs, 1 Soleus
Weekly volumes:
Chest - 8
Front Delts - 8

pages.text:
BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

his lifts daily. For the TRX exercises, he wants to increase their difficulty
by increasing the TRX band's length every time he trains. Because
Frank is still very sensitive to the training stimulus, and because of the
relatively high frequency of the program, we ask him to stay far away
from muscular failure in his sets (2-3 reps). If he does get into trouble
increasing the weight and hitting his benchmark rep target, we advise him
not to increase the weight, but to increase the amount of reps he does
with the previous session's weight.

Lastly, it's very important that Frank eats enough calories, all day every
day. Prepping meals to take with him to his internship is crucial, as is
eating high fat, slow digesting protein meals before bed. A weightgainer
supplement to get his protein up to 110 g+ (1.8 g/kg) per day might be
needed, seeing his seemingly low level of knowledge on proper nutrition.
headings: by increasing the TRX band’s length every time he trains. Because

sleeping), a push-pull-legs split workout program seems to be the best

week would allow him some flexibility for scheduling his training
sessions, and extra time for recuperating from the soreness that is
likely to happen during the first few weeks.

Quads - 8
Hamstrings - 8
Gastrocs - 10
Glutes - 10
Soleus - 10
Spinal Erectors - 8

This puts most of his muscles at the lower end of the 9-12 sets
recommendation for novices. Gastrocs and soleus a little higher because
they often need a little more volume, while some overemphasis on the
rear delts (as opposed to the front delts, which is often the case) with 12
sets/week is arguably a bad thing. The 10 sets per week for the Glutes are
an inevitable byproduct from Squatting and TRX Leg Curls, but are still
within the recommended range of 9-12 sets per week for untrained/novice
individuals.

Starting at very low starting weights, Frank wants to add 1-2.5 kg to all

his lifts daily. For the TRX exercises, he wants to increase their difficulty
by increasing the TRX band's length every time he trains. Because
Frank is still very sensitive to the training stimulus, and because of the
relatively high frequency of the program, we ask him to stay far away
from muscular failure in his sets (2-3 reps). If he does get into trouble
increasing the weight and hitting his benchmark rep target, we advise him
not to increase the weight, but to increase the amount of reps he does
with the previous session's weight.

Lastly, it's very important that Frank eats enough calories, all day every
day. Prepping meals to take with him to his internship is crucial, as is
eating high fat, slow digesting protein meals before bed. A weightgainer
supplement to get his protein up to 110 g+ (1.8 g/kg) per day might be
needed, seeing his seemingly low level of knowledge on proper nutrition.
